behaviors.description: Processes files placed in subdirectories of the watched root, not only files in the root directory
behaviors.justification: The failure occurs at the module boundary between filesystem watching and processing. The daemon must correctly configure recursive directory watching, which can only be verified with real filesystem structure. Mocking the file watcher would hide whether recursive watching is actually enabled—a mock would pass with an incorrect watcher setup.
behaviors.test_description: Create a nested subdirectory structure under the watched root (e.g., root/subdir/nested), place a file in the nested directory, start the daemon, and verify it processes the file as evidenced by output written to the output directory.

behaviors.description: Deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window
behaviors.justification: This is a deterministic algorithmic behavior—dedup logic that takes file path and timestamp as inputs and returns a pass/reject decision. No I/O, filesystem interaction, or module coordination is required. Test inputs (paths, timestamps) can be directly mocked as function parameters without hiding any real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test the dedup function: call it with the same file path twice, first within the dedup window (should return reject), then after the window expires (should return accept). Test boundary cases: exact window expiry time, different paths, same path different casing.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once
behaviors.justification: The failure is not just in dedup logic (which is Unit), but in how the daemon integrates event reception, deduplication, and processing under rapid conditions. A Unit test of dedup alone wouldn't verify that the daemon doesn't process a file multiple times before dedup rejects the duplicates. Real or realistically simulated rapid filesystem events are needed to expose this boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Rapidly write to the same file multiple times in quick succession (milliseconds apart) to simulate OS event flooding, then verify the daemon has processed the file exactly once by checking output directory for the processed result and confirming processing did not repeat (e.g., via log timestamps or idempotent marker).

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart
behaviors.justification: The failure is in error handling and recovery when real filesystem state changes occur. The daemon's ability to survive directory deletion and resume watching the recreated directory can only be verified with actual filesystem operations. Mocking the filesystem would hide failures in the real error recovery path (e.g., not re-registering the watcher after catching a delete error).
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon watching a directory, delete the directory while the daemon is running, immediately recreate it, place a test file in it, and verify the daemon processes the new file without requiring a manual restart or external intervention.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps
behaviors.justification: The failure is in sorting/ordering logic—a deterministic algorithm that takes a file list with timestamps and returns them in sorted order. This can be tested by mocking file metadata (creation timestamps) without filesystem I/O. The test verifies the ordering function produces the correct sort order; mocking file data is sufficient to catch sorting bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock list of files with creation timestamps in random order, call the ordering function, and assert that the output is sorted in ascending order by creation timestamp. Include edge cases: duplicate timestamps, very close timestamps (within milliseconds), and files with unusual timestamp values.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete
behaviors.justification: The failure is in write-completion detection, which depends on real file I/O behavior and timing. The daemon likely detects incomplete files using a stability check (no size change for N seconds) or OS-level lock detection; both require real file operations to verify correctly. Mocking file state would hide race conditions and timing bugs in the completion detection logic.
behaviors.test_description: Open a file handle and write to a file (keeping the handle open), trigger a daemon scan, and verify the file is not processed. Close the file, wait for stability period if applicable, trigger another scan, and verify the file is now processed.
